deprecated"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":36,"created_at":"2022-03-11T06:29:57Z","updated_at":"2024-12-01T01:36:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### _Please edit this tracker as necessary_

Below a list of deprecations by kind and scipy release. Ideally all _**deprecations should come with an announced behaviour change, and with an announced timeframe**_ (i.e. SciPy version where things will be changed/removed), and other items migrated there as appropriate.

In most cases, we aim for removal/behaviour change _one year_ after a given deprecation is introduced in `1.N` (not just in documentation, but in a way that the user cannot miss, i.e. raise DeprecationWarning upon use). Due to the 6 month release cadence this often ends up being `1.(N+2)`, but exceptional out-of-band releases can occur, so try to avoid promising exact versions. For more disruptive cases, adaptations (e.g. longer timeframes) can be made as necessary.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> If you add newly-deprecated functionality to the tracker, please do so _twice_ - once for tracking what we did for the upcoming release (whether following up on old doc-only deprecation or completely new deprecations), and once for tracking when that deprecation is scheduled to be executed (generally two releases later).

# Cleaning up the past

### Deprecations in documentation only

None currently tracked, if you find one, let us know!

### Deprecated without announced removal

None currently tracked, if you find one, let us know!

<!-- Sharpen warning to announce removal in 1.{N+1,N+2}, or remove directly in upcoming release, depending. -->

### Removal announced, missed

* 1.5 (announced removal for 1.7)
  * [ ] #15596
* 1.9 (announced removal for 1.11)
  * [ ] #15707

# In progress for upcoming release

> [!NOTE] 
> For removals and new or sharpened deprecations, please keep the  [release notes](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/wiki/*-Release-Note-Entries-for-SciPy-1.15.0-(asterisk-makes-this-appear-at-the-top)#expired-deprecations) up-to-date.

### Old deprecations, warning sharpened for removal in 1.16/1.17

None so far

### New deprecations

- [ ] #18703
- [x] #19223
- [x] finish #19907
- [x] #20558
- [x] #21085
- [x] #21157
- [x] #21211
- [x] #21633
- [x] #21572 
- [x] #21706
- [x] #21754
- [x] #21446

### Removal announced for 1.15

* 1.12 (removal intentionally delayed by one release due to out-of-band 1.13)
  * [x] #20867
  * [x] #20859
  * [x] #20866
  * [x] #20865
  * [x] #20864
  * [x] #21125 (follow-up to [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/19060) and incidental imports [clean-up](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/19904))
  * [x] #21759  (removal had been announced for 1.14)
* 1.13
  * [x] #20869 (follow-up to [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/commit/18bb04b95df98d5264bd3e522b8adde916f17e05))
  * [x] #20868
  * [x] https://github.com/scipy/scipy/commit/b19bcf36389bf2ef6f7a81229458ee73dd7cec86 (follow-up to [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/19716))

# Scheduled removals in future versions

### Removal announced for 1.16

<!-- Please formulate these as follow-up actions, not just links to a PR that does a deprecation (which should go under "New deprecations" for the upcoming release) -->

Open issues for these once 1.15 branches off of `main`.

* 1.14
  * remove `conjtransp()` from dok_array/matrix classes (follow-up to [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20283))
  * remove support for masked arrays from `power_divergence` and `chisquare` (follow-up to [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20753))
  * remove support for `quadrature='trapz'` from `integrate.quad_vec` (follow-up to [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20716))
  * raise error for non-integer types with `exact=True` in `special.comb` (follow-up to [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20780))
  * remove one-arg use for `stats.linregress` (follow-up to [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20789))
  * raise error for non-integer types with `exact=True` in `special.perm` (follow-up to [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20909))



### Removal announced for 1.17

<!-- Please formulate these as follow-up actions, not just links to a PR that does a deprecation (which should go under "New deprecations" for the upcoming release) -->

* 1.15
  * remove `rand` and `seed` functions from `scipy.linalg.interpolative` (follow-up to [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20558))
  * raise an error for complex inputs to `spatial.{cosine,correlation}` (follow-up to [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21085))
  * remove `scipy.stats.find_repeats` (follow-up to [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21157))
  * remove support for object arrays and longdoubles in `signal.{correlate,convolve,lfilter,sosfilter}` (follow-up to [this](gh-21211) and [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21706)
  * remove `scipy.linalg.kron` (follow-up to [this](gh-21633))
  * remove `kulczynski1` and `sokalmichener` metrics from `spatial.distance` (follow-up to [this](gh-21572))
  *  Remove ravelling of inputs in `linalg.toeplitz` (follow-up to [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21446))

### Removal announced/planned for 2.0

* all private modules affected by #14360 / #19907
* remove `scipy.misc` (follow-up to [this](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/15608))

# Other

### Outstanding deprecations

Describe your issue.

I'm having issues trying to get scipy-openblas to work properly for local development on macOS (as a prelude to using it in a CI entry).

Local usage
------------
Sonoma 14.2.1, M3
If I use the latest version of scipy-openblas (scipy_openblas32-0.3.26.0.4-py3-none-macosx_11_0_arm64.whl) I get a whole load of `ImportError: dlopen(/Users/andrew/Documents/Andy/programming/scipy/build-install/lib/python3.11/site-packages/scipy/linalg/_flapack.cpython-311-darwin.so, 0x0002)` errors, and if I run `otool -L` on the dylib the openblas library is not listed. Using `nm -a` on the dylib indicates that a lot of the BLAS symbols are unresolved.

If I drop back to `scipy-openblas32<=0.3.23.293.2` then the build works, but there are a couple of test fails

> FAILED scipy/special/tests/test_cdflib.py::test_nctdtr_gh19896 - AssertionError: 
FAILED scipy/special/tests/test_cdflib.py::test_nctdtrinc_gh19896 - AssertionError: 

If I repeat the tests on my home machine I get similar behaviour, but a lot more test fails. This might be due to slightly different compiler versions (I have gfortran 13.2.0 and clang-1500.1.0.2.5 on my M3)

CI usage
---------
If I do a similar process (using the pinned scipy-openblas wheel, see [here](https://github.com/andyfaff/scipy/pull/62/files) for run configuration) on a GHA CI using a `macos-14` image the build completes. However the tests reveal segfaults, e.g. [here](https://github.com/andyfaff/scipy/actions/runs/8003061306/job/21857572355#step:4:7668). These segfaults appear to come from tests involving linalg (i.e. OpenBLAS). Describe your issue.

I'm having issues trying to get scipy-openblas to work properly for local development on macOS (as a prelude to using it in a CI entry).

Local usage
------------
Sonoma 14.2.1, M3
If I use the latest version of scipy-openblas (scipy_openblas32-0.3.26.0.4-py3-none-macosx_11_0_arm64.whl) I get a whole load of `ImportError: dlopen(/Users/andrew/Documents/Andy/programming/scipy/build-install/lib/python3.11/site-packages/scipy/linalg/_flapack.cpython-311-darwin.so, 0x0002)` errors, and if I run `otool -L` on the dylib the openblas library is not listed. Using `nm -a` on the dylib indicates that a lot of the BLAS symbols are unresolved.

If I drop back to `scipy-openblas32<=0.3.23.293.2` then the build works, but there are a couple of test fails

> FAILED scipy/special/tests/test_cdflib.py::test_nctdtr_gh19896 - AssertionError: 
FAILED scipy/special/tests/test_cdflib.py::test_nctdtrinc_gh19896 - AssertionError: 

If I repeat the tests on my home machine I get similar behaviour, but a lot more test fails. This might be due to slightly different compiler versions (I have gfortran 13.2.0 and clang-1500.1.0.2.5 on my M3)

CI usage
---------
If I do a similar process (using the pinned scipy-openblas wheel, see [here](https://github.com/andyfaff/scipy/pull/62/files) for run configuration) on a GHA CI using a `macos-14` image the build completes. However the tests reveal segfaults, e.g. [here](https://github.com/andyfaff/scipy/actions/runs/8003061306/job/21857572355#step:4:7668). These segfaults appear to come from tests involving linalg (i.e. OpenBLAS). The specification for the Github actions M1 runners are [here](https://github.com/actions/runner-images/blob/main/images/macos/macos-14-arm64-Readme.md).

I'm wondering what the situation is for scipy-openblas w.r.t using it in CI? A pinned version seems to be fine for Linux.


@mattip

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->

Closes https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21660

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->

It removes the usage of `ld_classic` flag on macOS. I tested locally on my system (macOS with `Apple clang version 16.0.0 (clang-1600.0.26.4)`) and on CI (see https://github.com/czgdp1807/scipy/pull/10). It works fine and doesn't give any warnings. Tests also pass.

s://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21092.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21092.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Hello all,

I encountered an issue in the minimize function while working with differential evolution. For some members of the population, the direc1 value of the extrapolated point became zero without being caught by the check in `_linesearch_powell`, meaning the optimisation could not progress further.

It turns out this problem arises during the iteration of successive points in `_minimize_powell`: In each step, the current point is stored for comparison with the subsequent point, from which the direc1 value for the next iteration is calculated. While the f(x)-value of each point is correctly stored, the corresponding x-value is not stored at all if the condition fx > fx2 is fulfilled. That means the current point will be stored with the correct f(x)-value but with the wrong (previous) x-value.

In some circumstances it can now happen that in one iteration `_linesearch_powell` results in a point with  `2.0 * (fx - fval) > bnd`, meaning the function doesn't break out, but with `x - x1 = direc1 = 0`, meaning the optimisation cannot progress further.

This pull request fixes the storing of x-values, such that all points are stored correctly for comparison with subsequent points of the iteration. Additionally, a unit test is implemented to check the correct behaviour.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21092/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21092/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2519033332","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21092#issuecomment-2519033332","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21092","id":2519033332,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6WJWX0","user":{"login":"andyfaff","id":702934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcwMjkzNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/702934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff","html_url":"https://github.com/andyfaff","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-05T03:54:47Z","updated_at":"2024-12-05T03:54:47Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I've lost currency with the changeset here. Describe your issue.

I'm seeing a large number of fails on macOS 15.1 (Sequoia, ARM M1) using Accelerate. I don't see those fails with scipy-openblas32. This leads me to believe that there's a problem with Accelerate, or how we're using it.

I did this test in a new conda environment, did `git clean -xdf`, etc.

The test log is too long to insert here, you can find it in a gist [here](https://gist.github.com/andyfaff/b964e2ecbfe73ca5dcfbfe14adaed89a)

@tylerjereddy, do you have any issues with Sequoia?

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
# conda create -c conda-forge -n dev312 python=3.12
# conda activate dev312
# pip install numpy cython ninja meson pythran pybind11 doit click rich_click pydevtool

python dev.py build --with-accelerate
python dev.py -n test -s linalg
```


### Error message

```shell
N/A
```

Describe your issue.

I have developed a small `pyw` which is using `scipy.fft` internally. Recently, I have noticed that this small script stops running on Windows 11.

As a workaround, I have tried to redirect `stdout` and `stderr` before importing `scipy.fft`:
```py
if sys.executable.endswith('pythonw.exe'):
    sys.stdout = open(os.devnull, 'w')
    sys.stderr = open(os.path.join(os.getenv('TEMP'), 'stderr-' + os.path.basename(sys.argv[0])), 'w')
```
If so, the python script works well but no error message is captured.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import tkinter as tk
from scipy.fft import fft, fftfreq
root = tk.Tk()
root.mainloop()
```


### Error message

```shell
Precisely, there is no error message.
```

Describe your issue.

Could be a bug, but probably documentation.

I'm familiarising myself with `differentiate.jacobian`, and my first step is to differentiate a scalar valued function, i.e. $R^m \rightarrow R^1$. The following example shows my process from trying to follow the notes faithfully.

You can see from the stack trace that fvec ends up being sent a 3-D array, which is incongruent with with what the notes say should happen for evaluating the jacobian at a single point.

I assume that it can probably be done, but if so the documentation definitely needs expanding in this area.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.differentiate import jacobian

# a scalar valued function R^3 --> R^1
def f(x):
    assert np.size(x) == 3
    return np.sin(x[0]) + 5 * x[1] + np.log10(x[2])
 
v = f(np.array([2, 3, 4.]))
assert (np.isscalar(v))

# Notes say that "argument f must be vectorized to accept an array of shape (m, p)"

def fvec(x):
argument f must be vectorized to accept an array of shape (m, p). The first axis represents the 
 inputs of 
; the second is for evaluating the function at multiple points in a single call.

    # the notes section states "f must be vectorized to accept an array of shape (m, p).
    # The first axis represents the m inputs of f, the second is for evaluating the function
    #  at multiple points in a single call."
    assert x.shape[0] == 3     # m = x.shape[0] = 3
    assert x.ndim == 2         # x.shape hould be (m, p)
    p = x.shape[1]

    # let's call our scalar function. x.T should have length `p`.
    assert len(x.T) == p

    output = np.array([f(l) for l in x.T])
    # at this point output should have length `p`.
    assert len(output) == 3
 
    # documentation states that "argument f must return an array of shape (n, p)"
    # because our function is scalar the array it should return needs to be (1, 3)
    # this means we need to insert an axis at the start
    output = output[None, :]
    assert output.shape == (1, p)
    return output

jacobian(fvec, np.array([2, 3., 4.]))
```


### Error message

```shell
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[88], line 23
     20     assert output.shape == (1, p)

Describe your issue.

Could be a bug, but probably documentation.

I'm familiarising myself with `differentiate.jacobian`, and my first step is to differentiate a scalar valued function, i.e. $R^m \rightarrow R^1$. The following example shows my process from trying to follow the notes faithfully.

You can see from the stack trace that fvec ends up being sent a 3-D array, which is incongruent with with what the notes say should happen for evaluating the jacobian at a single point.

I assume that it can probably be done, but if so the documentation definitely needs expanding in this area.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.differentiate import jacobian

# a scalar valued function R^3 --> R^1
def f(x):
    assert np.size(x) == 3
    return np.sin(x[0]) + 5 * x[1] + np.log10(x[2])
 
v = f(np.array([2, 3, 4.]))
assert (np.isscalar(v))

# Notes say that "argument f must be vectorized to accept an array of shape (m, p)"

def fvec(x):
    # the notes section states "f must be vectorized to accept an array of shape (m, p).
    # The first axis represents the m inputs of f, the second is for evaluating the function
    #  at multiple points in a single call."
    assert x.shape[0] == 3     # m = x.shape[0] = 3
    assert x.ndim == 2         # x.shape hould be (m, p)
    p = x.shape[1]

    # let's call our scalar function. x.T should have length `p`.
    assert len(x.T) == p

    output = np.array([f(l) for l in x.T])
    # at this point output should have length `p`.
    assert len(output) == 3
 
    # documentation states that "argument f must return an array of shape (n, p)"
    # because our function is scalar the array it should return needs to be (1, 3)
    # this means we need to insert an axis at the start
    output = output[None, :]
    assert output.shape == (1, p)
    return output

jacobian(fvec, np.array([2, 3., 4.]))
```

Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

I want to minimize a function with ~ 1k parameters where I don't have access to analytic gradients and a single function evaluation is quite expensive (~ 5s), but is single threaded.

Hence one optimization step takes quite long, since most time is spent estimating the gradient. 

### Describe the solution you'd like.

I'd like an option to tell scipy to parallelize the function calls required for gradient estimation, e.g. 
`scipy.optimize.minimize(func, x0, jac="2-point-parallel")`

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

This is currently already possible with a callback `jac` argument, but I feel like this is a common pattern, which might be useful to include in scipy:

```python
def compute_partial_derivative(func, x, i, h):
    x_forward = np.array(x, copy=True)
    x_backward = np.array(x, copy=True)
    x_forward[i] += h
    x_backward[i] -= h
    return (func(x_forward) - func(x_backward)) / (2 * h)


def parallel_gradient(x, h=1e-8):
    grad = np.zeros_like(x)

    with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=20) as executor:
        futures = {executor.submit(compute_partial_derivative, func, x, i, h): i for i in range(len(x))}
        for future in futures:
            i = futures[future]
            grad[i] = future.result()

    return grad

res = scipy.optimize.minimize(func, x0, jac=parallel_gradient)
```

has to bear in mind that the internal finite differences is doing quite a lot of stuff, so is one reason why parallelisation hasn't been attempted.
These things include detailed step size estimation, obeying bounds, dealing with sparse and dense systems, 2 or 3 point differences, etc.

Something like the following should also work, using the new `differentiate.jacobian` functionality.

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.differentiate import jacobian
from multiprocess.dummy import Pool

def f_black_box(x):
    # I'm assuming that you can't vectorise within this function (you may not have access to it)
    # but you can parallelise its evaluation outside of it.
    return np.sin(x[0]) + 5 * x[1] + np.log10(x[2])

def parallel_g(func, pwl):
    
    def gradient(x):
        def fvec(x):
            shp = x.shape
            xtemp = np.reshape(x, (shp[0], -1))
                
            output = np.array(list(pwl.map(func, xtemp.T)))
            output = np.reshape(output, (1,) + shp[1:])
            return output
        
        res = jacobian(fvec, x)
        return res.df[0]

    return gradient

with Pool() as pwl:
    g = parallel_g(f_black_box, pwl)
    v = g(np.array([2., 3., 4.]))
    print(v)
```

If one wanted to get super fancy you can fiddle with the options to `jacobian` to reduce the number of evaluations required to get a good estimation. For example, let's estimate a good step size ahead of time:

```python
def _eps_for_method(x0_dtype, f0_dtype, method):

    # the default EPS value
    EPS = np.finfo(np.float64).eps

    x0_is_fp = False
    if np.issubdtype(x0_dtype, np.inexact):
        # if you're a floating point type then over-ride the default EPS
        EPS = np.finfo(x0_dtype).eps
        x0_itemsize = np.dtype(x0_dtype).itemsize
        x0_is_fp = True

    if np.issubdtype(f0_dtype, np.inexact):
        f0_itemsize = np.dtype(f0_dtype).itemsize
        # choose the smallest itemsize between x0 and f0
        if x0_is_fp and f0_itemsize < x0_itemsize:
            EPS = np.finfo(f0_dtype).eps

    if method in ["2-point", "cs"]:
        return EPS**0.5
    elif method in ["3-point"]:
        return EPS**(1/3)
    else:
        raise RuntimeError("Unknown step method, should be one of "
                           "{'2-point', '3-point', 'cs'}")


def compute_absolute_step(rel_step, x0, f0, method):
    """
    Computes an absolute step from a relative step for finite difference
    calculation.

    Parameters
    ----------
    rel_step: None or array-like
        Relative step for the finite difference calculation
    x0 : np.ndarray
        Parameter vector
    f0 : np.ndarray or scalar
    method : {'2-point', '3-point', 'cs'}

    """
    # this is used instead of np.sign(x0) because we need
    # sign_x0 to be 1 when x0 == 0.
    sign_x0 = (x0 >= 0).astype(float) * 2 - 1

    rstep = _eps_for_method(x0.dtype, f0.dtype, method)

    if rel_step is None:
        abs_step = rstep * sign_x0 * np.maximum(1.0, np.abs(x0))
    else:
        # User has requested specific relative steps.
        # Don't multiply by max(1, abs(x0) because if x0 < 1 then their
        # requested step is not used.
        abs_step = rel_step * sign_x0 * np.abs(x0)

        # however we don't want an abs_step of 0, which can happen if
        # rel_step is 0, or x0 is 0. Instead, substitute a realistic step
        dx = ((x0 + abs_step) - x0)
        abs_step = np.where(dx == 0,
                            rstep * sign_x0 * np.maximum(1.0, np.abs(x0)),
                            abs_step)

    return abs_step


def parallel_g(func, pwl):
    
    def gradient(x):
        def fvec(x):
            shp = x.shape
            xtemp = np.reshape(x, (shp[0], -1))
                
            output = np.array(list(pwl.map(func, xtemp.T)))
            output = np.reshape(output, (1,) + shp[1:])
            return output

        h = compute_absolute_step(None, x, np.float64(1.0), '3-point')

        res = jacobian(fvec, x, initial_step=h, maxiter=4, order=2)
        print(res)
        return res.df[0]

    return gradient


with Pool() as pwl:
    g = parallel_g(f_black_box, pwl)
    v = g(np.array([2., 3., 4.]))
    print(v)
```

Describe your issue.

Could be a bug, but probably documentation.

I'm familiarising myself with `differentiate.jacobian`, and my first step is to differentiate a scalar valued function, i.e. $R^m \rightarrow R^1$. The following example shows my process from trying to follow the notes faithfully.

You can see from the stack trace that fvec ends up being sent a 3-D array, which is incongruent with with what the notes say should happen for evaluating the jacobian at a single point.

I assume that it can probably be done, but if so the documentation definitely needs expanding in this area.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.differentiate import jacobian

# a scalar valued function R^3 --> R^1
def f(x):
    assert np.size(x) == 3
    return np.sin(x[0]) + 5 * x[1] + np.log10(x[2])
 
v = f(np.array([2, 3, 4.]))
assert (np.isscalar(v))

# Notes say that "argument f must be vectorized to accept an array of shape (m, p)"

def fvec(x):
    # the notes section states "f must be vectorized to accept an array of shape (m, p).
    # The first axis represents the m inputs of f, the second is for evaluating the function
    #  at multiple points in a single call."
    assert x.shape[0] == 3     # m = x.shape[0] = 3
    assert x.ndim == 2         # x.shape hould be (m, p)
    p = x.shape[1]

    # let's call our scalar function. x.T should have length `p`.
    assert len(x.T) == p

    output = np.array([f(l) for l in x.T])
    # at this point output should have length `p`.
    assert len(output) == 3
 
    # documentation states that "argument f must return an array of shape (n, p)"
    # because our function is scalar the array it should return needs to be (1, 3)
    # this means we need to insert an axis at the start
    output = output[None, :]
    assert output.shape == (1, p)
    return output

jacobian(fvec, np.array([2, 3., 4.]))
```

Describe your issue.

I'm experimenting with working in a certain multiprocessing context. I have an object in which I'd like to store an array namespace. However, array namespaces don't appear to be pickleable, which prevents my object being serialised to a pickle. I can work around this, but the workaround will require a large volume of calls to `array_namespace`.

- is there another way of storing the `array_namespace` which is pickleable?
OR
- can the namespace be altered to permit this kind of serialisation?

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy._lib._array_api import array_namespace
import pickle

x0 = np.array([1., 2, 3])
xp = array_namespace(x0)
pkl = pickle.dumps(xp)
```


### Error message

```shell
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[5], line 7
      5 x0 = np.array([1., 2, 3])
      6 xp = array_namespace(x0)
----> 7 pkl = pickle.dumps(xp)

TypeError: cannot pickle 'module' object
```

Describe your issue.

I'm experimenting with working in a certain multiprocessing context. I have an object in which I'd like to store an array namespace. However, array namespaces don't appear to be pickleable, which prevents my object being serialised to a pickle. I can work around this, but the workaround will require a large volume of calls to `array_namespace`.

- is there another way of storing the `array_namespace` which is pickleable?
OR
- can the namespace be altered to permit this kind of serialisation?

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy._lib._array_api import array_namespace
import pickle

x0 = np.array([1., 2, 3])
xp = array_namespace(x0)
pkl = pickle.dumps(xp)
```


### Error message

```shell
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[5], line 7
      5 x0 = np.array([1., 2, 3])
      6 xp = array_namespace(x0)
----> 7 pkl = pickle.dumps(xp)

TypeError: cannot pickle 'module' object
```

```shell

Describe your issue.

I'm experimenting with working in a certain multiprocessing context. I have an object in which I'd like to store an array namespace. However, array namespaces don't appear to be pickleable, which prevents my object being serialised to a pickle. I can work around this, but the workaround will require a large volume of calls to `array_namespace`.

- is there another way of storing the `array_namespace` which is pickleable?
OR
- can the namespace be altered to permit this kind of serialisation?

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy._lib._array_api import array_namespace
import pickle

x0 = np.array([1., 2, 3])
xp = array_namespace(x0)
pkl = pickle.dumps(xp)
```


### Error message

```shell
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[5], line 7
      5 x0 = np.array([1., 2, 3])
      6 xp = array_namespace(x0)
----> 7 pkl = pickle.dumps(xp)

TypeError: cannot pickle 'module' object
```


### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
HEAD
```

I faked a slow running objective function using `time.sleep`. If the objective function is fast then using parallelisation will lead to slowdown due to process overhead.

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import rosen
from scipy.optimize._numdiff import approx_derivative
from multiprocessing import Pool
import time
from stuff import fun2

# def fun2(x):
#     time.sleep(0.001)
#     return rosen(x)
```

```python
rng = np.random.default_rng()
```

```python
x0 = rng.uniform(high=10, size=(2000,))
f0 = rosen(x0)
```

```python
%timeit approx_derivative(fun2, x0, f0=f0)
```

    10.5 s ± 5.91 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)

```python
# create a map-like vectorised version
# note that the first argument to approx_derivative
# is effectively ignored in the way I've written it
def fun(f, x, *args, **kwds):
    xx = np.r_[[xs for xs in x]]
    return f(xx.T)

%timeit approx_derivative(fun2, x0, workers=fun, f0=f0)    
```

    91.8 ms ± 755 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10 loops each)

```python
elapsed = []
with Pool() as workers:
    for i in range(10):
        t = time.perf_counter()
        approx_derivative(fun2, x0, workers=workers.map, f0=f0)
        et = time.perf_counter()
        elapsed.append(et - t)

np.mean(elapsed)
```

    np.float64(1.442545195999901)

import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import rosen
from scipy.optimize._numdiff import approx_derivative
from multiprocessing import Pool
import time
from stuff import fun2

# def fun2(x):
#     time.sleep(0.0005)

rng = np.random.default_rng()
x0 = rng.uniform(high=10, size=(1000,))

elapsed = []
for i in range(10):
    t = time.perf_counter()
    _minimize_lbfgsb(fun2, x0, workers=map, maxfun=100000000)
    et = time.perf_counter()
    elapsed.append(et - t)
    print(et - t)

elapsed = []
with Pool() as workers:
    for i in range(10):
        t = time.perf_counter()
        _minimize_lbfgsb(fun2, x0, workers=workers.map, maxfun=100000000)
        et = time.perf_counter()
        print(et-t)
        elapsed.append(et - t)

np.mean(elapsed)
```

Timings are ~14 s for serial calculation, 2.5 s for parallel calculation on macOS M3 machine, (4 performance core, 4 efficiency core)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2561208671/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

does not accept lowest minimum if first iteration does not converge","user":{"login":"bc537","id":140304131,"node_id":"U_kgDOCFzfAw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/140304131?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bc537","html_url":"https://github.com/bc537","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bc537/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bc537/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bc537/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bc537/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bc537/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bc537/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bc537/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bc537/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bc537/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":37334597,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzMzNDU5Nw==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/defect","name":"defect","color":"e11d21","default":false,"description":"A clear bug or issue that prevents SciPy from being installed or used as expected"},{"id":37341011,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MTAxMQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/scipy.optimize","name":"scipy.optimize","color":"0366d6","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-12-27T03:58:08Z","updated_at":"2024-12-27T09:07:16Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi, I have recently tested out the basinhopping function on scipy 1.14.1 and I found that if the first iteration of basinhopping does not converge, it saves the results of the first minimum in lowest_optimization_result regardless if it found lower minima in subsequent iterations. Here's an example of the output (you might need to rerun the code multiple times to get something similar):

```
at minimum -0.7375 accepted 1 with coordinates [-0.62472279 -0.0292009 ]
at minimum -1.0096 accepted 1 with coordinates [-0.19491806 -0.13569248]
at minimum -0.7411 accepted 0 with coordinates [-0.62432619 -0.06236071]
at minimum -1.0105 accepted 1 with coordinates [-0.19507886 -0.11809183]
at minimum -0.4277 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 0.66336356 -0.03681024]
at minimum -0.7419 accepted 0 with coordinates [-0.62423137 -0.12444897]
at minimum -1.0107 accepted 1 with coordinates [-0.19514056 -0.08538831]
at minimum -0.9071 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 0.23408163 -0.11442866]
at minimum -0.9070 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 0.23417198 -0.08255703]
at minimum -1.0074 accepted 0 with coordinates [-0.19515293 -0.04109825]
at minimum -0.4316 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 0.66351317 -0.09399936]

 message: Maximum number of function evaluations has been exceeded.
 success: False
  status: 2
     fun: -0.7374566068964459
       x: [-6.247e-01 -2.920e-02]
    nfev: 200
   maxcv: 0.0
```

In this case the first minimum with value -0.7375 is saved since the maximum number of function evaluations has exceeded, and that takes priority even though a lowest minimum at -1.0107 has been found and converged correctly. Conversely, if the first iteration has converged then it would save the lower minimum's information, as expected:

```
at minimum -0.7375 accepted 1 with coordinates [-0.62472279 -0.0292009 ]
at minimum -0.4308 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 0.66336435 -0.07014512]
at minimum -0.4303 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 0.66340108 -0.13661439]
at minimum -0.7380 accepted 1 with coordinates [-0.62429375 -0.03294593]
at minimum -0.9072 accepted 1 with coordinates [ 0.23416975 -0.10742507]
at minimum -1.0109 accepted 1 with coordinates [-0.19514945 -0.09631739]
at minimum 0.4179 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 1.09260812 -0.14401779]
at minimum -1.0105 accepted 0 with coordinates [-0.19501635 -0.12027818]
at minimum -1.0107 accepted 0 with coordinates [-0.19507442 -0.08633754]
at minimum 0.4166 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 1.09249345 -0.12516556]
at minimum -0.4316 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 0.66328278 -0.10706697]
 message: Optimization terminated successfully.
 success: True
  status: 1
     fun: -1.010861911064749
       x: [-1.951e-01 -9.632e-02]
    nfev: 138
   maxcv: 0.0
```

I guess one can implement a callback function for this but a project I'm working on that requires this function also has a wrapper that uses a callback function in itself, so I feel it would be best if this is rectified within the function itself. Thank you. 

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy.optimize import basinhopping
import numpy as np

minimizer_kwargs = {"method":"COBYLA", "jac":False, "options":{'maxiter':200}}
x0 = [1.0, 1.0]

def func2d(x):
    f = np.cos(14.5 * x[0] - 0.3) + (x[1] + 0.2) * x[1] + (x[0] +
                                                           0.2) * x[0]
    df = np.zeros(2)
    df[0] = -14.5 * np.sin(14.5 * x[0] - 0.3) + 2. * x[0] + 0.2
    df[1] = 2. * x[1] + 0.2
    return f

def print_fun(x, f, accepted):
        print("at minimum %.4f accepted %d" % (f, int(accepted)), "with coordinates", x)

rng = np.random.default_rng()
ret = basinhopping(func2d, x0, minimizer_kwargs=minimizer_kwargs,
                   niter=10, callback=print_fun, seed=rng, T=0)

print(ret.lowest_optimization_result)

information"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-12-27T03:58:08Z","updated_at":"2024-12-27T11:24:25Z","closed_at":"2024-12-27T11:24:25Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi, I have recently tested out the basinhopping function on scipy 1.14.1 and I found that if the first iteration of basinhopping does not converge, it saves the results of the first minimum in lowest_optimization_result regardless if it found lower minima in subsequent iterations. Here's an example of the output (you might need to rerun the code multiple times to get something similar):

```
at minimum -0.7375 accepted 1 with coordinates [-0.62472279 -0.0292009 ]
at minimum -1.0096 accepted 1 with coordinates [-0.19491806 -0.13569248]
at minimum -0.7411 accepted 0 with coordinates [-0.62432619 -0.06236071]
at minimum -1.0105 accepted 1 with coordinates [-0.19507886 -0.11809183]
at minimum -0.4277 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 0.66336356 -0.03681024]
at minimum -0.7419 accepted 0 with coordinates [-0.62423137 -0.12444897]
at minimum -1.0107 accepted 1 with coordinates [-0.19514056 -0.08538831]
at minimum -0.9071 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 0.23408163 -0.11442866]
at minimum -0.9070 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 0.23417198 -0.08255703]
at minimum -1.0074 accepted 0 with coordinates [-0.19515293 -0.04109825]
at minimum -0.4316 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 0.66351317 -0.09399936]

 message: Maximum number of function evaluations has been exceeded.
 success: False
  status: 2
     fun: -0.7374566068964459
       x: [-6.247e-01 -2.920e-02]
    nfev: 200
   maxcv: 0.0
```

In this case the first minimum with value -0.7375 is saved since the maximum number of function evaluations has exceeded, and that takes priority even though a lowest minimum at -1.0107 has been found and converged correctly. Conversely, if the first iteration has converged then it would save the lower minimum's information, as expected:

```
at minimum -0.7375 accepted 1 with coordinates [-0.62472279 -0.0292009 ]
at minimum -0.4308 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 0.66336435 -0.07014512]
at minimum -0.4303 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 0.66340108 -0.13661439]
at minimum -0.7380 accepted 1 with coordinates [-0.62429375 -0.03294593]
at minimum -0.9072 accepted 1 with coordinates [ 0.23416975 -0.10742507]
at minimum -1.0109 accepted 1 with coordinates [-0.19514945 -0.09631739]
at minimum 0.4179 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 1.09260812 -0.14401779]
at minimum -1.0105 accepted 0 with coordinates [-0.19501635 -0.12027818]
at minimum -1.0107 accepted 0 with coordinates [-0.19507442 -0.08633754]
at minimum 0.4166 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 1.09249345 -0.12516556]
at minimum -0.4316 accepted 0 with coordinates [ 0.66328278 -0.10706697]
 message: Optimization terminated successfully.
 success: True
  status: 1
     fun: -1.010861911064749
       x: [-1.951e-01 -9.632e-02]
    nfev: 138
   maxcv: 0.0
```

I guess one can implement a callback function for this but a project I'm working on that requires this function also has a wrapper that uses a callback function in itself, so I feel it would be best if this is rectified within the function itself. Thank you. 

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy.optimize import basinhopping
import numpy as np

minimizer_kwargs = {"method":"COBYLA", "jac":False, "options":{'maxiter':200}}
x0 = [1.0, 1.0]

def func2d(x):
    f = np.cos(14.5 * x[0] - 0.3) + (x[1] + 0.2) * x[1] + (x[0] +
                                                           0.2) * x[0]
    df = np.zeros(2)
    df[0] = -14.5 * np.sin(14.5 * x[0] - 0.3) + 2. * x[0] + 0.2
    df[1] = 2. * x[1] + 0.2
    return f

def print_fun(x, f, accepted):
        print("at minimum %.4f accepted %d" % (f, int(accepted)), "with coordinates", x)

rng = np.random.default_rng()
ret = basinhopping(func2d, x0, minimizer_kwargs=minimizer_kwargs,
                   niter=10, callback=print_fun, seed=rng, T=0)

print(ret.lowest_optimization_result)

Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

The performance of the `_ConstraintWrapper` in the `_differentialevolution` module tends to suffer for many calls to the constraint function. After some profiling, it seems like `np.atleast_2d()` has noticeable overhead. 

### Describe the solution you'd like.

The solution seems straightforward enough: 

Simply replace (lines 1888:1895)
```
    ...
    elif isinstance(constraint, LinearConstraint):
      def fun(x):
        if issparse(constraint.A):
          A = constraint.A
        else:
          A = np.atleast_2d(constraint.A)
        
        res = A.dot(x)
    ...
```

With

```
    ...
    elif isinstance(constraint, LinearConstraint):
      if issparse(constraint.A):
        A = constraint.A
      else:
        A = np.atleast_2d(constraint.A)
      def fun(x):
        res = A.dot(x)
      ...
```

The matrix `A` never changes, so there should be no issue with changing the implementation to this for some performance benefit.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

_No response_

Describe your issue.

Hello, 

I've encountered an issue with the minimize method from scipy.optimize. I've reduced my complex problem to a minimal example. Can anyone confirm this is a bug?
In the example, we have 5 points in the x-y plane. The start and end point are fixed. The 3 remaining intermediate points are varied in their y value with the objective and constraint function. 

**Objective**: The total length between start and end point via the intermediate points.
**Constraint**: The intermediate points must not be in the forbidden area in the middle (= their y value must not be in the range from -3 to +3).

**Behaviour**: The optimizer converges to a result, where the points are on a straight line, leading to the highest possible constraint violation value (which is 3).

**Expected behaviour**: At least, the two points, which at the initial guess are outside the forbidden area, should not be sucked into it. In the best case, all the intermediate points should end outside the forbidden area.

If the initial guess starts in a state, where the constraint is fulfilled, everything works fine.


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
# This is a minimal example for the nonlinear contraints and scipy's optimize method

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from scipy.optimize import minimize, NonlinearConstraint

x = np.array([0, 1, 2, 3, 4])

# All outside constraint - Works
# y_init = np.array([0, -5, -5, -5, 0])

# One inside constraint - Does not work
y_init = np.array([0, -5, -2, -5, 0])
y_inner = y_init[1:-1]

def constraints(y_inner):
    """Returns amount of y values that are in the invalid area"""
    
    constrained_height = 3.0

    violations = 0
    for i in range(len(y_inner)):
        if abs(y_inner[i]) <= constrained_height:
            violations += 1
    
    return violations

# Define the nonlinear constraint function. The constraint is fulfilled if no y values are in the invalid area.
nlc = NonlinearConstraint(constraints, 0, np.inf)

def energy_objective(y_inner):
    global y_init, x
    """Returns the objective (length of the path)"""

    # Combine start, inner and end points to complete path
    y = np.concatenate(([y_init[0]], y_inner, [y_init[-1]]))

    # Calculate differences between consecutive points
    diffs_x = x[1:] - x[:-1]
    diffs_y = y[1:] - y[:-1]

    # Return total path length using Euclidean distance

Describe your issue.

Hello, 

I've encountered an issue with the minimize method from scipy.optimize. I've reduced my complex problem to a minimal example. Can anyone confirm this is a bug?
In the example, we have 5 points in the x-y plane. The start and end point are fixed. The 3 remaining intermediate points are varied in their y value with the objective and constraint function. 

**Objective**: The total length between start and end point via the intermediate points.
**Constraint**: The intermediate points must not be in the forbidden area in the middle (= their y value must not be in the range from -3 to +3).

**Behaviour**: The optimizer converges to a result, where the points are on a straight line, leading to the highest possible constraint violation value (which is 3).

**Expected behaviour**: At least, the two points, which at the initial guess are outside the forbidden area, should not be sucked into it. In the best case, all the intermediate points should end outside the forbidden area.

If the initial guess starts in a state, where the constraint is fulfilled, everything works fine.


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
# This is a minimal example for the nonlinear contraints and scipy's optimize method

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from scipy.optimize import minimize, NonlinearConstraint

x = np.array([0, 1, 2, 3, 4])

# All outside constraint - Works
# y_init = np.array([0, -5, -5, -5, 0])

# One inside constraint - Does not work
y_init = np.array([0, -5, -2, -5, 0])
y_inner = y_init[1:-1]

def constraints(y_inner):
    """Returns amount of y values that are in the invalid area"""
    
    constrained_height = 3.0

    violations = 0
    for i in range(len(y_inner)):
        if abs(y_inner[i]) <= constrained_height:
            violations += 1
    
    return violations

# Define the nonlinear constraint function. The constraint is fulfilled if no y values are in the invalid area.
nlc = NonlinearConstraint(constraints, 0, np.inf)

def energy_objective(y_inner):
    global y_init, x
    """Returns the objective (length of the path)"""

    # Combine start, inner and end points to complete path
    y = np.concatenate(([y_init[0]], y_inner, [y_init[-1]]))

    # Calculate differences between consecutive points
    diffs_x = x[1:] - x[:-1]
    diffs_y = y[1:] - y[:-1]

    # Return total path length using Euclidean distance
    return np.sum(np.sqrt(diffs_x**2 + diffs_y**2))

# Optimize path using trust-region constrained algorithm
result = minimize(
    energy_objective, 
    y_inner, # Initial guess for inner points
    method='trust-constr',
    constraints=nlc,
    options={
        # 'verbose': 3,  # Uncomment for detailed optimization output
        'maxiter': 50,
        }
    )

# Get optimized path by combining start, optimized inner points and end
y_optimized = np.concatenate(([y_init[0]], result.x, [y_init[-1]]))
print("y_optimized:", y_optimized)

# Plot the points and the constraint area

# Example output:
# - Shows initial path (blue) and optimized path (red)
# - Gray rectangle shows forbidden area
# - Optimized path should avoid gray area while minimizing total length

plt.plot(x, y_init, 'ro', label="Initial", color='blue')
plt.plot(x, y_init, '-')
plt.plot(x, y_optimized, 'ro', label="Optimized", color='red')
plt.plot(x, y_optimized, '-')
plt.fill_between([0.5, 3.5], -3, 3, color='gray', alpha=0.5)
plt.grid(True)
plt.legend()
plt.xlabel("x")
plt.ylabel("y")
plt.show()
```

Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

The performance of the `_ConstraintWrapper` in the `_differentialevolution` module tends to suffer for many calls to the constraint function. After some profiling, it seems like `np.atleast_2d()` has noticeable overhead. 

### Describe the solution you'd like.

The solution seems straightforward enough: 

Simply replace (lines 1888:1895)
```
    ...
    elif isinstance(constraint, LinearConstraint):
      def fun(x):
        if issparse(constraint.A):
          A = constraint.A
        else:
          A = np.atleast_2d(constraint.A)
        
        res = A.dot(x)
    ...
```

With

```
    ...
    elif isinstance(constraint, LinearConstraint):
      if issparse(constraint.A):
        A = constraint.A
      else:
        A = np.atleast_2d(constraint.A)
      def fun(x):
        res = A.dot(x)
      ...
```

The matrix `A` never changes, so there should be no issue with changing the implementation to this for some performance benefit.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

_No response_

Describe your issue.

Having issues building (locally) on Ubuntu 22.04, looking to update some of the CI for GPU testing.
This is done in a fresh docker container. The there's a whole bunch of error messages, but they all seem to be related to c++17/c++14 parameter passing.

g++ versio

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
apt update
cd src
apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends python3-dev libopenblas-dev pkg-config gfortran build-essential python3.10-venv
python3 -m venv ../venvs/scipy-venv
source ../venvs/scipy-venv/bin/activate
python -m pip install build
python -m build . --wheel
g++ --version
```

```python

python -m pip install build
python -m build . --wheel
g++ --version

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->

This PR unifies the workflows to produce free-threaded wheels with the non-free-threaded ones. This will reduce maintenance burden regarding dependency updates, as well as platform-specific tweaks required to compile and package SciPy.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
1. Right now, Windows wheels on `cp313t` are not available due to a Python detection bug in Meson.~ → This was fixed by https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson/pull/13851
2. Wheels are being successfully being built on my local fork: https://github.com/andfoy/scipy/actions/runs/11615618486

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->

This PR unifies the workflows to produce free-threaded wheels with the non-free-threaded ones. This will reduce maintenance burden regarding dependency updates, as well as platform-specific tweaks required to compile and package SciPy.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
1. Right now, Windows wheels on `cp313t` are not available due to a Python detection bug in Meson.~ → This was fixed by https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson/pull/13851
2. Wheels are being successfully being built on my local fork: https://github.com/andfoy/scipy/actions/runs/11615618486

I appreciate where you're coming from.

One difficulty with those constrained tests is that some of them are very slow to run. This is not great when running them in continuous integration. This is why the `popsize` is low. 
The main purpose of the test is to know that DE has found the correct minimum, whilst still obeying the bounds and 
constraints. If polishing is disabled one would have to figure out a different (more relaxed) tolerance criterion.

I have just double checked this locally. The polishing step is not masking the underlying DE having found the wrong solution. The testing asserts all succeed if polishing is turned off, but I did have to increase `popsize` and decrease `tol` to 0.01. 
In the end the polishing makes it easier to figure out if the solve worked, because it's likely to be closer to the known solution.

CI tests revealed that various COBYQA tests were taking a long time. Local testing reveals that there is a lot of overhead in COBYQA internals compared to the time taken to call the objective function. I believe that there are inefficiencies in the implementation that need to be discovered and reduced.
The example below shows that only 0.2% of time is spent in the objective function for COBYQA, whereas for nelder-mead it's 33%. COBYQA gets to the solution in a smaller number of nfev (nelder-mead doesn't actually get to a good minimum), but it takes two orders of magnitude more time.

@ragonneau 

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import rosen, differential_evolution, minimize
bounds = [(0, 10)] * 10
rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
x0 = rng.random(size=10)*10

start = time.time()
res =  minimize(rosen, x0, method='cobyqa')
finish = time.time()
sftime = time.time()
_ = [rosen(res.x) for i in range(res.nfev)]
fftime = time.time()

print((fftime - sftime)/(finish - start), res.nfev, finish-start)

start = time.time()
res =  minimize(rosen, x0, method='nelder-mead')
finish = time.time()
sftime = time.time()
_ = [rosen(res.x) for i in range(res.nfev)]
fftime = time.time()

print((fftime - sftime)/(finish - start), res.nfev, finish-start)
```

0.30101082386617767 1898 0.02931809425354004
```

I think I've fixed my problem, and it wasn't a scipy issue. I've been using (brew installed) podman as a replacement for docker.

I started off by trying to git bisect in the podman, but couldn't even build really old tags. I then reinstalled docker and could do a successful HEAD build in ostensibly the same container, with the same commands (note - I still see the build noise outlined in the original post).

I then did a search for `c++: fatal error: Killed signal terminated program cc1plus` and there were several posts indicating that this could happen if the build process was using too many resources (e.g. out of memory, too much parallelisation).

This lead me to think that the podman machine didn't have enough resources by default, but docker probably does. I gave the podman machine more resources by:

```
podman machine stop
podman machine set --cpus 8 --memory 8192
podman machine start
podman run -it -v $PWD:/src ubuntu:22.04
```

Following that reconfiguration the build works. I suspect it was mainly a memory problem. By default the podman machine is set to 2048 MB.

Thus, the moral of the story is, if you're trying to build in a container using podman/docker, the container needs to be given enough resources.

Feel free to close. I didn't close to give people a chance to read the post.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2458129287/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

gh-21823 , gh-21812, gh-21774 

#### What does this implement/fix?
As part of the [SPEC 7](https://scientific-python.org/specs/spec-0007/) transition effort, several PRs are applying the `_transition_to_rng` decorator to existing functions. Current practice is to manually replace the old `seed`/`random_state` docstring with a temporary `rng` entry, which documents both old and new keyword behavior. This is over 20 lines long, and it requires that `seed`/`random_state` needs to be adjusted manually depending on the old keyword name. Besides being tedious to copy-paste, edit, and review for correctness (e.g. I found a copy-paste mistake in one of them), it will need to be manually replaced when the `_transition_to_rng` decorator is removed.

This PR allows the `_transition_to_rng` to automatically replace the documentation of the `rng` parameter with the desired boilerplate. Now, when applying `_transition_to_rng` decorator to functions, the documentation of `seed`/`random_state` only needs to be changed to the [recommened](https://scientific-python.org/specs/spec-0007/#implementation) *final* wording:
```
    rng : `numpy.random.Generator`, optional
        Pseudorandom number generator state. When `rng` is None, a new
        `numpy.random.Generator` is created using entropy from the
        operating system. Types other than `numpy.random.Generator` are
        passed to `numpy.random.default_rng` to instantiate a ``Generator``.
```
which is identical for every function. This way, no adjustments are needed when the decorator is +    rng : {None, int, `numpy.random.Generator`}, optional
+        If `rng` is passed by keyword, types other than `numpy.random.Generator` are
+        passed to `numpy.random.default_rng` to instantiate a ``Generator``.
+        If `rng` is already a ``Generator`` instance, then the provided instance is
+        used. Specify `rng` for repeatable interpolation.
+
+        If this argument `random_state` is passed by keyword,
+        legacy behavior for the argument `random_state` applies:
+
+        - If `random_state` is None (or `numpy.random`), the `numpy.random.RandomState`

+        - If `random_state` is an int, a new ``RandomState`` instance is used,
+          seeded with `random_state`.
+        - If `random_state` is already a ``Generator`` or ``RandomState`` instance then
+          that instance is used.
+

Describe your issue.

If I try to import anything from scipy.stats, or scipy.stats itself, I get `RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded`.

I include a minimal example below, but have confirmed that this bug occurs whether I import scipy and try to run a stats package (i.e., `import scipy; scipy.stats.norm.rvs()`, or if I try to import scipy.stats itself (`import scipy.stats as st`) or one of its distributions (`from scipy.stats import norm`).

Included error message is reproduced from a Jupyter Notebook for brevity, but has been confirmed in a standard python terminal.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
`import scipy.stats as st`
```

gh-21823

#### What does this implement/fix?
Currently, the `_transition_to_rng` decorator replaces the entirety of a function's `rng` decumentation with boilerplate, but sometimes additional information is desired. This PR changes the logic so that only the first paragraph is replaced; anything after the first blank line is preserved.

#### Additional information
For example, in gh-21823, we'd like for this text to be included in the `rng` description:

> Specify `rng` for repeatable minimizations. The random numbers generated only affect the visiting distribution function and new coordinates generation.

but currently it is [omitted](https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.dual_annealing.html). Compare against [docs rendered with this PR](https://output.circle-artifacts.com/output/job/d0b49c36-573f-4a84-aafd-d024f7f349bb/artifacts/0/html/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.dual_annealing.html).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21853/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21853/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2466454504","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21853#issuecomment-2466454504","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21853","id":2466454504,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6TAxvo","user":{"login":"andyfaff","id":702934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcwMjkzNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/702934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff","html_url":"https://github.com/andyfaff","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-09T20:45:08Z","updated_at":"2024-11-09T20:45:08Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Looks like it renders correctly. Reference issue
Toward gh-21833

#### What does this implement/fix?
I combined the two functions in `scipy.stats._resampling` into one PR because it seemed like a logical unit. I'll need to do the two classes `BootstrapMethod` and `PermutationMethod` in a separate PR because they have attributes `random_state` that may require additional attention.

#### Additional information
I'm noticing that a lot of uses of `random_state` need to remain in these files because they use old distributions. Most of these are just the normal distribution, though, so it would be easy enough to come back and change these to use the new infrastructure so use of `rng` is more consistent (especially in documentation).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21854/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21854/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2466459633","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21854#issuecomment-2466459633","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21854","id":2466459633,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6TAy_x","user":{"login":"andyfaff","id":702934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcwMjkzNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/702934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff","html_url":"https://github.com/andyfaff","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-09T21:05:05Z","updated_at":"2024-11-09T21:05:05Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> I'm noticing that a lot of uses of random_state need to remain in these files because they use old distributions.

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->

Linked PR from `spin` - https://github.com/scientific-python/spin/pull/238
~https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27606~

Regarding the context, we are moving away from `dev.py` and shifting to `spin` for building, testing SciPy. Right now it's a work in progress. I have completed `spin build`, `spin test` and `spin docs`. Apologies for the delay in providing background for this PR. Its in a good shape now. Testing on CI still remains to be done.

Sub commands check list,

- [x] `build`
- [x] `test`
- [ ] `lint`
- [ ] `mypy`
- [ ] `shell`
- [ ] `python`
- [ ] `ipython`
- [x] `docs`
- [ ] `refguide-check`
- [ ] `smoke-docs`
- [ ] `bench`
- [ ] `smoke-tutorials`

What needs to be done to merge,

- [ ] Implement as many of the sub-commands without touching `spin`. Only reviews will be addressed in https://github.com/scientific-python/spin/pull/238 from now onwards.
- [ ] Test everything on CI here once https://github.com/scientific-python/spin/pull/238 is in and a new release is available.
- [ ] Address all the reviews and questions.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
cc: @melissawm @rgommers 

Steps to reproduce the change here and in https://github.com/scientific-python/spin/pull/238,

1. Clone `spin` locally into your system. `git clone https://github.com/scientific-python/spin`.
2. Change your working directory to `spin` (cloned above).
3. Activate your `scipy` environment. For example I use `scipy-dev` from `conda`. So I do, `conda activate scipy-dev`.
4. Fetch my branch from https://github.com/scientific-python/spin/pull/238. I basically do, `git remote add czgdp1807 https://github.com/czgdp1807/spin`. `get fetch czgdp1807 spin_upd`. `czgdp1807` is the remote pointing to my fork.
5. Checkout to `spin_upd` branch (just fetched above).
6. Do an editable install of `spin`. I do, `pip install -e .`.
7. Now switch to SciPy directory in your system.
8. Fetch `spin_build` (this PR) from my fork. Checkout to `spin_build`.
9. Activate the same SciPy environment where you did the editable install of `spin` (in step 6).
10. Describe your issue.

I'm seeing a large number of fails on macOS 15.1 (Sequoia, ARM M1) using Accelerate. I don't see those fails with scipy-openblas32. This leads me to believe that there's a problem with Accelerate, or how we're using it.

I did this test in a new conda environment, did `git clean -xdf`, etc.

The test log is too long to insert here, you can find it in a gist [here](https://gist.github.com/andyfaff/b964e2ecbfe73ca5dcfbfe14adaed89a)

@tylerjereddy, do you have any issues with Sequoia?

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
# conda create -c conda-forge -n dev312 python=3.12
# conda activate dev312
# pip install numpy cython ninja meson pythran pybind11 doit click rich_click pydevtool

python dev.py build --with-accelerate
python dev.py -n test -s linalg
```


### Error message

```shell
N/A
```

Describe your issue.

I'm seeing a large number of fails on macOS 15.1 (Sequoia, ARM M1) using Accelerate. I don't see those fails with scipy-openblas32. This leads me to believe that there's a problem with Accelerate, or how we're using it.

I did this test in a new conda environment, did `git clean -xdf`, etc.

The test log is too long to insert here, you can find it in a gist [here](https://gist.github.com/andyfaff/b964e2ecbfe73ca5dcfbfe14adaed89a)

@tylerjereddy, do you have any issues with Sequoia?

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
# conda create -c conda-forge -n dev312 python=3.12
# conda activate dev312
# pip install numpy cython ninja meson pythran pybind11 doit click rich_click pydevtool

python dev.py build --with-accelerate
python dev.py -n test -s linalg
```

```

example, if you try to compute first order derivative with first order finite difference formula. To do it you need function values only in two points: ``f(x-dx)`` and ``f(x+dx)``. But ``scipy.misc.derivative`` calculates it three times because of this line: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/c3fa90dcfcaef71658744c73578e9e7d915c81e9/scipy/misc/common.py#L94 
Zero in the middle of ``weights`` list causes computations of ``f(x)`` that is actually not used.
To me  it seems like completely unnecessary 50% slowdown.

Zero in the middle of ``weights`` list causes computations of ``f(x)`` that is actually not used.
To me  it seems like completely unnecessary 50% slowdown.

Describe your issue.

I'm seeing a large number of fails on macOS 15.1 (Sequoia, ARM M1) using Accelerate. I don't see those fails with scipy-openblas32. This leads me to believe that there's a problem with Accelerate, or how we're using it.

I did this test in a new conda environment, did `git clean -xdf`, etc.

The test log is too long to insert here, you can find it in a gist [here](https://gist.github.com/andyfaff/b964e2ecbfe73ca5dcfbfe14adaed89a)

@tylerjereddy, do you have any issues with Sequoia?

# conda create -c conda-forge -n dev312 python=3.12
# conda activate dev312
# pip install numpy cython ninja meson pythran pybind11 doit click rich_click pydevtool

python dev.py build --with-accelerate
python dev.py -n test -s linalg
```

#### Reference issue

See https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/20669

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR adds an additional CI job on top of the already-existing 3.13 free threaded one, this new CI pipeline runs the SciPy testsuite using the [pytest-run-parallel](https://github.com/Quansight-Labs/pytest-run-parallel/) plugin, which allows to run individual tests concurrently during a pytest session

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->

Several tests were using the global random number generator, via `np.random.seed`, which was causing numeric result mismatches when the tests were being run in parallel. Instead, all tests now use `np.random.RandomState`, which ensures that each thread gets its own independent random I'm seeing a large number of fails on macOS 15.1 (Sequoia, ARM M1) using Accelerate. I don't see those fails with scipy-openblas32. This leads me to believe that there's a problem with Accelerate, or how we're using it.

I did this test in a new conda environment, did `git clean -xdf`, etc.

The test log is too long to insert here, you can find it in a gist [here](https://gist.github.com/andyfaff/b964e2ecbfe73ca5dcfbfe14adaed89a)

@tylerjereddy, do you have any issues with Sequoia?

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
# conda create -c conda-forge -n dev312 python=3.12
# conda activate dev312
# pip install numpy cython ninja meson pythran pybind11 doit click rich_click pydevtool

python dev.py build --with-accelerate
python dev.py -n test -s linalg
```

```

`test_kmeans2_kpp_low_dim` and `test_kmeans2_kpp_high_dim` I couldn't work out how to get the test to pass using default_rng

Yes, this kind of thing is annoying!

Possible ameliorations:

- instead of setting global state use `seed=np.random.RandomState(42)` and pass that instance to `kmeans2`. This postpones the effort until the deprecation period is over.
- fiddle with `default_rng` seed until tests pass.
- delve into the original reported issue that caused the test to be added and code to be changed, understand the problem, see if the fix is correct, see if the test uses random number generation appropriately, etc.

I think this process is revealing several locations of where the original fixes/tests (probably latter) weren't thought through correctly - they simply shouldn't rely on seeds being certain values.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2472075163/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I think the method for each type shuffles slices independently, and if `indices` is an array the shuffling is done on a copy.

Also, I think `Generator.permute(indices, axis=-1)` and `Generator.permutation(indices, axis=-1)` do the same thing. It's not clear to me why there are two Generator methods doing the same thing.

If `RandomState.permutation` is slow then that's probably justification for sticking with status quo.

Reference issue
Toward gh-21833

#### What does this implement/fix?
I combined the two functions in `scipy.stats._resampling` into one PR because it seemed like a logical unit. I'll need to do the two classes `BootstrapMethod` and `PermutationMethod` in a separate PR because they have attributes `random_state` that may require additional attention.

#### Additional information
I'm noticing that a lot of uses of `random_state` need to remain in these files because they use old distributions. Most of these are just the normal distribution, though, so it would be easy enough to come back and change these to use the new infrastructure so use of `rng` is more consistent (especially in Reference issue
Toward gh-21833

#### What does this implement/fix?
I combined the two functions in `scipy.stats._resampling` into one PR because it seemed like a logical unit. I'll need to do the two classes `BootstrapMethod` and `PermutationMethod` in a separate PR because they have attributes `random_state` that may require additional attention.

#### Additional information
I'm noticing that a lot of uses of `random_state` need to remain in these files because they use old distributions. Most of these are just the normal distribution, though, so it would be easy enough to come back and change these to use the new infrastructure so use of `rng` is more consistent (especially in Reference issue
Toward gh-21833

#### What does this implement/fix?
I combined the two functions in `scipy.stats._resampling` into one PR because it seemed like a logical unit. I'll need to do the two classes `BootstrapMethod` and `PermutationMethod` in a separate PR because they have attributes `random_state` that may require additional attention.

#### Additional information
I'm noticing that a lot of uses of `random_state` need to remain in these files because they use old distributions. Most of these are just the normal distribution, though, so it would be easy enough to come back and change these to use the new infrastructure so use of `rng` is more consistent (especially in Reference issue
Toward gh-21833

#### What does this implement/fix?
I combined the two functions in `scipy.stats._resampling` into one PR because it seemed like a logical unit. I'll need to do the two classes `BootstrapMethod` and `PermutationMethod` in a separate PR because they have attributes `random_state` that may require additional attention.

#### Additional information
I'm noticing that a lot of uses of `random_state` need to remain in these files because they use old distributions. Most of these are just the normal distribution, though, so it would be easy enough to come back and change these to use the new infrastructure so use of `rng` is more consistent (especially in Reference issue
Toward gh-21833

#### What does this implement/fix?
I combined the two functions in `scipy.stats._resampling` into one PR because it seemed like a logical unit. I'll need to do the two classes `BootstrapMethod` and `PermutationMethod` in a separate PR because they have attributes `random_state` that may require additional attention.

#### Additional information
I'm noticing that a lot of uses of `random_state` need to remain in these files because they use old distributions. Most of these are just the normal distribution, though, so it would be easy enough to come back and change these to use the new infrastructure so use of `rng` is more consistent (especially in documentation).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21854/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21854/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2472390365","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21854#issuecomment-2472390365","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21854","id":2472390365,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6TXa7d","user":{"login":"andyfaff","id":702934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcwMjkzNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/702934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff","html_url":"https://github.com/andyfaff","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyfaff/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-13T05:03:14Z","updated_at":"2024-11-13T05:03:14Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I think the PR is mergeable as-is.

However, I think it's also worth the time modifying the tests a.la. #21872, i.e.

1. Removing all occurrences of `np.random.seed`
2. Removing all usages of the global `RandomState` that generate test data (e.g. [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/57e210a9b977141fa7c7a5183184fe96de58a7a4/scipy/stats/tests/test_resampling.py#L86)) and converting them to use a specific instance of a rng.
3. When 2 is carried out, the specific instance may as well be a `Generator`.

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
See https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/20669

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR adds an additional CI job on top of the already-existing 3.13 free threaded one, this new CI pipeline runs the SciPy testsuite using the [pytest-run-parallel](https://github.com/Quansight-Labs/pytest-run-parallel/) plugin, which allows to run individual tests concurrently during a pytest session

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->

Several tests were using the global random number generator, via `np.random.seed`, which was causing numeric result mismatches when the tests were being run in parallel.
